elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \begin{tikzpicture} \node at (0,0) {\includegraphics[width=300pt]{./Elisa2.pdf}}; \coordinate [label=above:\textcolor{black}{{$z$}}] (theta) at (0.3,3.2); \coordinate [label=above:\textcolor{black}{{$x$}}] (theta) at (5,-0.8); \coordinate [label=above:\textcolor{black}{{$y$}}] (theta) at (-3,-3.4); \coordinate [label=above:\textcolor{black}{{$P$}}] (theta) at (-5.3,1.6); \coordinate [label=above:\textcolor{black}{{ $dM$}}] (theta) at (-2.65,-1.75); \coordinate [label=above:\textcolor{black}{{ $\varphi$}}] (theta) at (-0.55,-1); \coordinate [label=above:\textcolor{black}{{ $\rho$}}] (theta) at (-0.9,-1.6); \coordinate [label=above:\textcolor{black}{{ $r$}}] (theta) at (-2.2,0.4); \coordinate [label=above:\textcolor{black}{{ $\Delta$}}] (theta) at (-3.5,-0.5); \end{tikzpicture} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate the qualitative characteristics of a test particle attracted to an irregular elongated body, modeled as a non-homogeneous straight segment with a variable linear density.
elements.sent-2411-002: By deriving the potential function in closed form, we formulate the Hamiltonian equations of motion for this system.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our analysis reveals a family of periodic circular orbits parameterized by angular momentum.
elements.sent-2411-004: Additionally, we utilize the axial symmetry resulting from rotations around the segment's axis to consider the corresponding reduced system.
elements.sent-2411-005: This approach identifies several reduced-periodic orbits by analyzing appropriate Poincar\'e sections.
elements.sent-2411-006: These periodic orbits are then reconstructed into quasi-periodic orbits within the full dynamical system.
elements.sent-2411-007: Understanding periodic orbits in non-uniform gravitational fields is essential for grasping the dynamic behaviors around asteroids, as well as for the engineering considerations involved in deep space exploration.
elements.sent-2411-008: The complexity arises from the diverse mass distributions of asteroids, which necessitates various models to describe their irregular gravitational fields.
elements.sent-2411-009: Common approaches include polyhedral models \cite{WernerScheeres1996 , mascon models \cite{GEISSLER1996140 , and simplified representations, such as straight segments for elongated bodies \cite{Duboshin1959, Prieto1994, Riaguas1999, Najid2011 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: To our knowledge, the potential of a non-homogeneous straight segment with variable linear density has not been thoroughly explored.
elements.sent-2411-011: While the potential for a homogeneous segment has been detailed in classical works such as MacMillan \cite{MacMillan1958 and Kellogg \cite{Kellogg1967 , with closed-form solutions found in \cite{Duboshin1959, Riaguas1999 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The present paper fills the gap by deriving the closed-form potential for variable density.
elements.sent-2411-013: It also provides a Hamiltonian formulation and examines the existence of periodic and quasi-periodic orbits, offering an efficient model for elongated bodies with asymmetric mass distributions, as an alternative to polyhedral or mascon models.
elements.sent-2411-014: The quadratic density model analyzed in \cite{Najid2011, Najid2012 assumes a symmetric mass distribution about the center of mass.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Consequently, this model is limited to representing bodies with symmetric mass distributions.
elements.sent-2411-016: In contrast, asymmetric mass distributions cannot be captured by such a model.
elements.sent-2411-017: This limitation leaves the dipole model \cite{2018AJ and our proposed linear density model as the only viable options for accurately modeling elongated bodies with asymmetric mass distributions, making them more suitable for a wider range of applications.}
elements.sent-2411-018: While various models have been proposed to represent the gravitational field of elongated bodies, comprehensive comparisons between them remain scarce.
elements.sent-2411-019: Notably, only one study \cite{2018AJ has directly compared the gravitational potentials of a straight segment with constant density and the dipole model, highlighting the strengths and limitations of each approach.}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, further research is required to compare additional models, such as those involving quadratic or linear densities, in terms of accuracy and computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-021: Such comparisons are crucial for advancing our understanding of particle dynamics near irregularly shaped celestial bodies, particularly in the context of asteroid exploration missions.
elements.sent-2411-022: We believe that this task requires a dedicated research, which will be addressed in a future work.
elements.sent-2411-023: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-024: In Section~\ref{Potential , we introduce the mathematical model of a non-homogeneous straight segment with variable linear density, deriving the closed-form expression for the gravitational potential.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Section \ref{Formulation-Newtonian-Hamiltonian provides the Hamiltonian formulation of the system.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Section~\ref{sec:PeriodicOrbits and Section~\ref{sec:QuasiPeriodicOrbits are dedicated to the study of periodic orbits, including circular orbits, and the reconstruction of quasi-periodic orbits from the reduced system.}}
elements.section-2411-002: Potential of a straight segment with linear density
elements.sent-2411-027: We fix an inertial reference frame $\{O;x,y,z\}$ with the $2L$-long segment $\mathcal S$ lying along the $x$-axis and centered at the origin as in the left image of Figure~\ref{fig:SegmentoFijoPosicionCentrado .}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this frame, we assume that the density is given by the linear function $\sigma(x)=\alpha x + \beta,\, x\in[-L,L]$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, our analysis will hinge on the following distances \begin{equation}\Delta= |\vec{ P Q}|,\quad r_{1}= |\vec{P E_1}|,\quad r_{2}= |\vec{PE_2}|,\end{equation} where $ \mathcal{P =(x,y,z)$ denotes a massless particle, $ \mathcal{Q}\in\mathcal S$ is arbitrary, and $E_1= (L,0,0)$ and $E_2=(-L,0,0)$ are the end points of the segment.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Keeping in mind that the total mass $M$ of the segment and the density are always positive, we obtain the following restrictions on the parameters.
elements.sent-2411-032: The total mass $M$ of the segment is given by $M=2L\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-033: The coordinates of the center of mass are $CM=\left(\bar{c ,0,0\right)$, where $\bar{c =\frac{2 \alpha L^3 {3 M}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The slope of the density function satisfaces: $- \frac{M {2L^2 < \alpha < \frac{M {2L^2 .$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The total mass $M$ of the segment and the $x$-coordinate $\bar{c}$ of the center of mass are given respectively by \begin{equation}M = \int_{-L}^L \sigma(x)\, dx,\quad \bar{c}= \frac{1}{M}\int_{-L}^L x\,\sigma(x) dx,\end{equation} which, after imposing $\sigma(x)=\alpha x + \beta$, lead to { (i) and { (ii) .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Item { (iii) follows from solving $\sigma(x)=\alpha x + \beta>0,$ with $\beta=M/2L$ and $ x\in [-L,L].$.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=0.46]{./Images/SegmentoFijoEjeBisX.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.5]{./Images/SF.pdf} \caption{ The fixed segment in different reference frames. Left: The reference frame $\{O;x,y,z\}$ with the segment centered on the origin and lying along the $x$-axis. Right: The new frame $\{O;u,v,w\}$ results from a translation in the $x$-axis locating the center of mass at the new origin.} \label{fig:SegmentoFijoPosicionCentrado}
elements.sent-2411-037: In what follows, we will restrict our study to positive values of the parameter $\alpha$, since the negative values are obtained just by switching the orientation of the $u$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-038: The computation of the potential function is done in a reference frame $\{O;u,v,w\}$ where the segment lies along the $u$-axis with its center of mass located at the origin, see the right image of Figure~\ref{fig:SegmentoFijoPosicionCentrado .}
elements.sent-2411-039: In this frame, the distances from $\mathcal{P}$ to the end points are \begin{equation}r_{1}= \sqrt{(-L +u+\bar{c})^2+v^2 + w^2},\quad r_{2}= \sqrt{(L + u+ \bar{c})^2+v^2 + w^2},\end{equation} and the density function is $\sigma(u) =\alpha u +\beta+\alpha \bar{c}$, with $ u\in[-L-\bar{c ,L-\bar{c ].$ Hence, the potential function is given by the following line integral \begin{equation}\begin{array}{rl} V(\mathcal{P}) = -G \displaystyle\int_\mathcal{S} \dfrac{d{\textrm M}}{\Delta}= -G \displaystyle\int_{-L-\bar c}^{L-\bar c} \dfrac{\sigma(u)\ du}{|\vec{ \mathcal{P} \mathcal{Q}}|}. \end{array}\end{equation} To simplify the denominator, we express $\vec{ \mathcal{P \mathcal{Q = r_1 + s\,r_{12}$, where $r_{12 =r_2 -r_1=2L$ with $s\in(0,1)$, which leads us to $|\vec{ \mathcal{P \mathcal{Q |=\sqrt{r_1^2 + s^2\,r_{12 ^2+2s\, r_1 r_{12 }$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}{rl} V(P) = -G \displaystyle\int_S \dfrac{d{\textrm M}}{\Delta}= -G \displaystyle\int_{-L-\bar c}^{L-\bar c} \dfrac{\sigma(u)\ du}{|\vec{ P Q}|}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-040: The above integral is computed, in terms of the new variable $s$, by considering the change of variable $u=2Ls-L- \bar{c}$, $du=2Lds$.
elements.sent-2411-041: Thus, considering the relation $2 r_1 r_{12 =r_2^2 -r_1^2 -4L$ obtained from the Cosine Theorem, we are left with the following integral \begin{equation}\begin{array}{rl} V(\mathcal P) = -G \displaystyle\int_0^1 \frac{c_1s +c_2 }{\sqrt{s^2+c_3 s+c_4}} \ ds, \end{array}\end{equation} where $c_1= 2L\alpha$, $c_2= \beta-\alpha L $, $c_3= (-4 L^2-{r _{1 ^2+{r _{2 ^2)/4 L^2$ and $c_4= {r _{1 ^2/4 L^2$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The final expression for the potential function is \begin{equation}\label{eq:PotencialOriginal3} V(u, v, w)=\frac{ c_1 G}{2 L} ( r_{1}- r_{2})-\frac{ G}{8 L^2} \left[ c_{1} \left(4 L^2+ r_{1}^2- r_{2}^2\right)+ 8c_{2} L^2\right] \ln \left(\frac{2 L+{r_1}+{r_2}}{-2 L+{r_1}+{r_2}}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{eq:PotencialOriginal3} V(u, v, w)=\frac{ c_1 G}{2 L} ( r_{1}- r_{2})-\frac{ G}{8 L^2} \left[ c_{1} \left(4 L^2+ r_{1}^2- r_{2}^2\right)+ 8c_{2} L^2\right] \ln \left(\frac{2 L+{r_1}+{r_2}}{-2 L+{r_1}+{r_2}}\right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-043: Notice, that $c_1=0$ leads us to the potential associated with constant density, see \cite{Kellogg1967,Duboshin1959, Riaguas1999 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Moreover, we highlight that $V$ only depends on the distances $ r_1$ and $ \mathbf r_2$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Therefore, the potential function remains the same under rigid transformations of the reference frame.
elements.sent-2411-046: For our subsequent analysis, we introduce the conjugate momenta $p=(p_u,p_v,p_w)$ associated with $q=(u,v,w)$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Then, the equations of motion can be written as a Hamiltonian system \begin{equation}\dot{q}=H_p,\quad \dot{p}=-H_q,\nonumber\end{equation} where $H(q,p;\alpha, M, L)$ is a multiparametric family of Hamiltonian functions given by \begin{equation} \label{hamiltoniano-rotando1} H(q,p)= \frac{1}{2} \left(p_u^2+p_v^2+p_w^2\right) +V(q), \end{equation} with $V(q)$ the potential function given in \eqref{eq:PotencialOriginal3 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: The domain of the Hamiltonian $H$ is $ \mathbb{R ^3 \setminus \mathcal{S}\times\mathbb{R ^3$, being $\mathcal{S = \{q\in \mathbb{R ^3 : u \in [-L-\bar{c , L-\bar{c ], \ v=0, \ w=0 \}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: To reduce the number of parameters, we customize the units through the following symplectic change of scale $(q,p)\to(Q,P)$, and time reparametrization \begin{equation}\label{scaling} q\to L Q,\quad p\to \sqrt{\frac{G M}{2 L}} P, \quad \alpha \to A (3 M)/2 L^2,\quad t=\frac{2 L}{G M}\tau,\end{equation} where $Q=(\xi,\eta,\zeta)$, $P=(p_\xi,p_\eta,p_\zeta)$.
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\label{scaling} q\to L Q,\quad p\to \sqrt{\frac{G M}{2 L}} P, \quad \alpha \to A (3 M)/2 L^2,\quad t=\frac{2 L}{G M}\tau,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-050: This transformation provides the new family of Hamiltonians depending on one parameter, $\mathcal H(Q,P)=\mathcal H(Q,P;A)$, given by \begin{equation} \label{eq:HamiltonianaEscalada} \mathcal H(Q,P)= \frac{1}{2} \left(p_\xi^2+p_\eta^2+p_\zeta^2\right) +U(Q;A), \end{equation} with $0\leqslant A\leqslant{1 /{3}$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: The domain of $\mathcal H$ is $\mathbb{R ^3 \setminus \mathcal{S}\times\mathbb{R ^3 $, where $\mathcal{S = \{Q \in \mathbb{R ^3 : \xi \in [-1-A, 1-A], \ \eta=0, \ \zeta=0 \}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Moreover, the potential is obtained through the following compact expression \begin{equation}\label{eq:PotencialEscalado} U(Q;A)=3 A \,d-\frac{1}{4}(3 A \,d\, s+4) \ln \left(\frac{s+2}{s-2}\right),\end{equation} where the auxiliary variables $s$ and $d$ are given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:VariablesAuxiliares} s=R_1+R_2,\quad d=R_1-R_2,\end{equation} with $R_1=\sqrt{( -1+A+\xi)^2+\eta ^2+\zeta ^2}$, and $R_2=\sqrt{(1+A+\xi )^2+\eta ^2+\zeta ^2 .$ The use of the auxiliary variables $s$ and $d$ will facilitate the analytic study by allowing much more compact expressions.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Furthermore, these variables satisfy the following properties.
elements.sent-2411-054: The variables $s$ and $d$ satisfy the following properties:.
elements.sent-2411-055: After the scaling, the longitude of the segment is equal to two.
elements.sent-2411-056: Hence, the particle $\mathcal{P}$ and end points $E_1$ and $E_2$ generate a triangle with sides equal to $R_1$, $R_2$, and 2.
elements.sent-2411-057: According to triangle inequality and the reverse triangle inequality we have \begin{equation}s=R_1+R_2>2,\quad |d|= |R_1-R_2|< 2 .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-058: Hence, the properties of the lemma hold.
elements.sent-2411-059: After all these manipulations, we may give the equations of motion in the following compact form \begin{equation} \label{eq:EqMovSegmentoFijo} \dot{\xi} = p_\xi ,\qquad \dot{p_\xi} = \frac{4\,(4d+12 A\, s)}{s^2-d^2}-3 A \ln \left(\frac{s+2}{s-2}\right), \dot{\eta} = p_\eta,\qquad \dot{p}_\eta =16\,\eta\,\frac{3 A\, d+s}{\left(s^2-4\right) \left(d^2-s^2\right)}, \dot{\zeta} = p_\zeta,\qquad \dot{p}_\zeta =-16\,\zeta\,\frac{3 A\, d+s}{\left(s^2-4\right) \left(s^2-d^2\right)}. \end{equation} Notice that the above system \eqref{eq:EqMovSegmentoFijo is invariant under rotations about $\xi$-axis.}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation} \label{eq:EqMovSegmentoFijo} \dot{\xi} = p_\xi ,\qquad \dot{p_\xi} = \frac{4\,(4d+12 A\, s)}{s^2-d^2}-3 A \ln \left(\frac{s+2}{s-2}\right), \dot{\eta} = p_\eta,\qquad \dot{p}_\eta =16\,\eta\,\frac{3 A\, d+s}{\left(s^2-4\right) \left(d^2-s^2\right)}, \dot{\zeta} = p_\zeta,\qquad \dot{p}_\zeta =-16\,\zeta\,\frac{3 A\, d+s}{\left(s^2-4\right) \left(s^2-d^2\right)}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-060: Moreover, all the planes that contain the segment are invariant.
elements.sent-2411-061: In particular, the $\xi$-axis, given by $\eta=\zeta=0$ and $p_\eta=p_\zeta=0$, is invariant.
elements.sent-2411-062: Due to the axial symmetry of the problem, it is convenient to define the following symplectic transformation $(\xi,\eta, \zeta, p_\xi,p_\eta, p_\zeta) \rightarrow (r, \theta , x, P_r , P_\theta, P_x)$ \begin{equation} \label{hamiltoniano-fijo-cilindricas} \xi= \,x,\quad p_\xi= P_x, \eta= \,r \cos\theta, \quad p_\eta= (r P_r \sin_\theta + P_{\theta} \cos\theta)/r, \zeta= \,r \sin\theta, \quad p_\zeta= (r P_r \cos\theta- P_{\theta}\sin\theta)/r. \end{equation} Thus, the Hamiltonian function in cylindrical coordinates is expressed as \begin{equation}\mathcal H=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(P_r^2 + P_x^2+ \dfrac{ P_\theta^2}{r^2}\right)+ 3 A \,d-\frac{1}{4}(3 A \,d\, s+4) \ln \left(\frac{s+2}{s-2}\right),\end{equation} and the equations of motion are the following \begin{equation} \label{sistema-ham-segmento-fijo-densidad-lineal1} \dot{r} = P_r,\qquad \dot{P_r} =\frac{P_\theta^2}{r^3}-\frac{16r(3 A d+s)}{(s^2-d^2)(4-s^2) }, \dot{\theta} = P_\theta/r^2,\qquad \dot{P_\theta} =0, \dot{x} = P_x,\qquad \dot{P_x} = \frac{4\,(4d+12A\, s)}{s^2-d^2}-3 A \ln \left(\frac{s+2}{s-2}\right). \end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation} \label{hamiltoniano-fijo-cilindricas} \xi= \,x,\quad p_\xi= P_x, \eta= \,r \cos\theta, \quad p_\eta= (r P_r \sin_\theta + P_{\theta} \cos\theta)/r, \zeta= \,r \sin\theta, \quad p_\zeta= (r P_r \cos\theta- P_{\theta}\sin\theta)/r. \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}\mathcal H=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(P_r^2 + P_x^2+ \dfrac{ P_\theta^2}{r^2}\right)+ 3 A \,d-\frac{1}{4}(3 A \,d\, s+4) \ln \left(\frac{s+2}{s-2}\right),\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation} \label{sistema-ham-segmento-fijo-densidad-lineal1} \dot{r} = P_r,\qquad \dot{P_r} =\frac{P_\theta^2}{r^3}-\frac{16r(3 A d+s)}{(s^2-d^2)(4-s^2) }, \dot{\theta} = P_\theta/r^2,\qquad \dot{P_\theta} =0, \dot{x} = P_x,\qquad \dot{P_x} = \frac{4\,(4d+12A\, s)}{s^2-d^2}-3 A \ln \left(\frac{s+2}{s-2}\right). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-063: The Hamiltonian system \eqref{sistema-ham-segmento-fijo-densidad-lineal1 has no equilibria.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Thus, the equation for $\dot{P_r}$ becomes \begin{equation}\frac{16r(3 A d+s)}{(s^2-d^2)(4-s^2) }=0,\end{equation} whose numerator is always strictly positive.
elements.section-2411-004: Periodic orbits of the non-homogeneous fixed straight segment
elements.sent-2411-066: Since the polar angle is cyclic in system \eqref{sistema-ham-segmento-fijo-densidad-lineal1 , we investigate the existence of periodic orbits on the reduced system obtained after disregarding the variables $(\theta,P_\theta)$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Note that the presence of $P_\theta$ in the following equations of motion plays the role of an arbitrary constant parameter, which from now on we denote $P_\theta=c$ \begin{equation} \label{sistema-ham-segmento-fijo-densidad-lineal1-reducido} \dot{r} = P_r,\qquad \dot{P_r} =\frac{c^2}{r^3}-\frac{16r(3 A d+s)}{(s^2-d^2)(4-s^2) }, \dot{x} = P_x,\qquad \dot{P_x} = \frac{4\,(4d+12A\, s)}{s^2-d^2}-3 A \ln \left(\frac{s+2}{s-2}\right). \end{equation} The inverse relation between the pairs $(r,x)$ and $(s,d)$ follows by solving the equations for $r$ and $x$, where now the radius in cylindrical coordinates are \begin{equation} \label{variables cilindricas-s-d} d= r_1-r_2=\sqrt{(A+x-1)^2+r^2}-\sqrt{(A+x+1)^2+r^2}, s= r_1+r_2=\sqrt{(A+x-1)^2+r^2}+\sqrt{(A+x+1)^2+r^2}. \end{equation} Hence, we obtain the following relation that will be useful in the study of the equilibria for the system \eqref{sistema-ham-segmento-fijo-densidad-lineal1-reducido \begin{equation}\label{relacion r-x-s-d} r(s,d)=\frac{1}{4} \sqrt{\left(s^2-4\right)\left(4-d^2\right) },\quad x(s,d)=-A-\frac{d s}{4}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation} \label{sistema-ham-segmento-fijo-densidad-lineal1-reducido} \dot{r} = P_r,\qquad \dot{P_r} =\frac{c^2}{r^3}-\frac{16r(3 A d+s)}{(s^2-d^2)(4-s^2) }, \dot{x} = P_x,\qquad \dot{P_x} = \frac{4\,(4d+12A\, s)}{s^2-d^2}-3 A \ln \left(\frac{s+2}{s-2}\right). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-068: After making some arrangements in \eqref{sistema-ham-segmento-fijo-densidad-lineal1-reducido comes down to study the roots of the following two functions in $s$ and $d$ \begin{equation} \label{sistema-ham-reducido-segmento-densidad-lineal3} F_1 =\left(d^2-4\right)^2 \left(s^2-4\right) (3 A d+s)+16 c^2 \left(d^2-s^2\right), F_2 =3 A s+d+\frac{3}{4} A \left(d^2-s^2\right) \ln \left(\frac{s+2}{s-2}\right). \end{equation} Since $r=r(s,d)$ and $x=x(s,d)$ from \eqref{relacion r-x-s-d , if we found a solution $(s^*,d^*)$ in \eqref{sistema-ham-reducido-segmento-densidad-lineal3 , then we obtain a critical point $(r(s^*,d^*),x(s^*,d^*),0,0)$ of the system \eqref{sistema-ham-segmento-fijo-densidad-lineal1-reducido .}}}}
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation} \label{sistema-ham-reducido-segmento-densidad-lineal3} F_1 =\left(d^2-4\right)^2 \left(s^2-4\right) (3 A d+s)+16 c^2 \left(d^2-s^2\right), F_2 =3 A s+d+\frac{3}{4} A \left(d^2-s^2\right) \ln \left(\frac{s+2}{s-2}\right). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-069: In \cite{Riaguas1999 , the author proved the existence of a circular orbit in an invariant perpendicular plane to the segment through the origin for the case $A=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-070: This orbit is obtained for \begin{equation}s_0=\frac{1}{2} \left(c^2+\sqrt{c^4+16}\right), \quad d_0=0,\quad P_\theta=c, \quad P_r=P_x=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-071: The following result shows that a unique circular solution exists for all values of parameter $A$ small enough.
elements.sent-2411-072: For $A$ small enough, there exist a unique periodic circular orbit \begin{equation}s=s(A)=s_0+\mathcal O(A^2), \quad d=d(A)=d'(0)\,A+\mathcal O(A^2),\quad P_\theta=c,\quad P_r=P_x=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-073: For this purpose, we consider the function $F(s,d; A)=(F_1, F_2)$ and employ the implicit function theorem to prove the existence of a unique differentiable functions $s(A)$ and $d(A)$, satisfying $F(s(A),d(A); A)=0$ in a small neighborhood of $A=0$.
elements.sent-2411-074: First, note that $F(s_0,0,0)=0$ and second, computing \begin{equation}DF(s_0,0;0)=\left({cc} \dfrac{\partial F_1}{\partial s} \dfrac{\partial F_1}{\partial d} [1.4ex]\dfrac{\partial F_2}{\partial s} \dfrac{\partial F_2}{\partial d}\right)_{A=0,d=0,s_0}=\left({cc} 8 \left(c^4+\sqrt{c^4+16} c^2+16\right) 0 [1.4ex] 0 1\right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-025: \begin{equation}DF(s_0,0;0)=\left({cc} \dfrac{\partial F_1}{\partial s} \dfrac{\partial F_1}{\partial d} [1.4ex]\dfrac{\partial F_2}{\partial s} \dfrac{\partial F_2}{\partial d}\right)_{A=0,d=0,s_0}=\left({cc} 8 \left(c^4+\sqrt{c^4+16} c^2+16\right) 0 [1.4ex] 0 1\right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-075: Thus, since $det|DF(s_0,0;0)|= 8 \left(c^4+\sqrt{c^4+16 c^2+16\right)\neq 0$, by the implicit function theorem, we conclude the proof.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Moreover, we may ensure the existence of the circular orbit for a wider domain of the parameter $A$.
elements.sent-2411-077: More precisely, we regard the equation $F_2$ as a second-degree polynomial in the variable $d$.
elements.sent-2411-078: Thus, assuming $A\neq0$ in $F_2$ and solving in the variable $d$, we obtain.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[scale=0.5]{Images/d+} \includegraphics[scale=0.45]{Images/d-} \caption{ Left and right figures shows the surfaces $d_+(s;A)$ and $d_-(s;A)$, for $s\in(2,100)$ and the parameter $A\in[0,1/3)$. Green and blue planes are $d=2$ and $d=-2$ respectively. In the domain of these figures we observe the brown surface, $d_+(s)\in(0,2)$ and the yellow plane, $d_-(s)\in(-\infty,-2)$.} \label{fig:dMm}
elements.sent-2411-079: Note that, since $A\in[0,1/3)$ and $s\in(2,+\infty)$ the radicand is always a positive number, hence $d_{\pm (s; A)\in \mathbb{R}$.}

elements.section-2411-005: Quasi-periodic orbits around a non-homogeneous fixed straight segment
elements.sent-2411-097: In the previous section, we obtained periodic orbits with constant $r(t)=r_0$ and $x(t)=x_0$.
elements.sent-2411-098: In this section, we will search for periodic orbits in the reduced space $(r,x,P_r,P_x)$.
elements.sent-2411-099: The 3-dimensional reconstruction of these reduced-periodic orbits leads to quasi-periodic orbits of the full system in $(r,\theta,x,P_r,P_\theta,P_x)$, unless any commensurability condition is imposed among the momenta.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[scale=0.18]{./Images/DensidadLineal18_035.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.18]{./Images/DensidadLineal18_07.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.18]{./Images/DensidadLineal18_1.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.18]{./Images/DensidadLineal18_18.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.18]{./Images/DensidadLineal14_035.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.18]{./Images/DensidadLineal14_07.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.18]{./Images/DensidadLineal14_1.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.18]{./Images/DensidadLineal14_18.pdf} \caption{ Poincar\'e sections for $A \neq 0$: First and second rows with $A = 1/8,\,1/4$, respectively. In both cases, from left to right, $c = 0.35,\, 0.7,\,1,\, 1.8$. These sections are constructed using the unit scaling defined in \eqref{scaling}.} \label{fig:SeccionesPoincareA14}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[scale=0.372]{./Images/DensidadLineal18_07OrbPeriodica.pdf} \includegraphics[scale=0.372]{./Images/DensidadLineal18_1Bis.pdf} \caption{ The relative equilibria $E_i$ for $i=1,\ldots,5$ correspond with examples of quasi-periodic orbits for $A=1/8$. From left to right, we consider $c=0.7$ and $c=1$ respectively.} \label{fig:SeccionesPoincareQuasiPO}
elements.sent-2411-100: Reduced-periodic orbits will be detected through suitable Poincar\'e sections, allowing us to analyze the influence of the parameter $A$ and the integral $P_\theta$ in the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-101: Precisely, the case $A=0$ was studied in \cite{RiaguasElipeLara1999 , where the authors detected several quasi-periodic orbits.}

From left to right, we consider $c=0.7$ and $c=1$ respectively.} \label{fig:SeccionesPoincareQuasiPObis}

elements.sent-2411-104: Regardless of the value of $A$, these sections show a well-organized toroidal structure for high values of $P_\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-105: As the integral $P_\theta$ decreases, topological changes in the phase space are embodied through islands, elliptic and hyperbolic points, and an increasing chaotic area.
elements.sent-2411-106: We continue denoting $P_\theta=c$ as in the above section.
